text: Opening of the @Scale Systems & Reliability 2026 conference.

Vice President of Engineering for Core Infrastructure at Meta presents the latest progress on infrastructure for AI through agentic innovation, spanning custom silicon, networking fabrics, and AI-driven system management.

Explores enabling agentic access to Meta systems safely, covering internal agents, third-party agent deployment, and the guardrails required to prevent destructive actions while granting agents real capability.
Argues that LLMs can manage infrastructure through general-purpose programming languages rather than bespoke DSLs, and discusses reliably mapping code changes to infrastructure outcomes.
Details Meta's integration of public cloud resources with internal infrastructure, focusing on multi-cloud optionality, GPU capacity management, and fleet management across environments.
Live audience Q&A on safely unleashing agents, agentic infrastructure, and extending Meta to the public cloud.
Describes a reliability flywheel that encodes on-call engineer investigation methods into an autonomous agent.

The agent handled 1,000+ incidents with a 60% improvement in detection-to-mitigation time and matched senior engineer performance roughly 80% of the time, covering pattern identification, autonomous investigation, supervised mitigation, and self-healing goals.
Covers the NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 system, an exascale computer within a single rack, including topology-aware scheduling, GPU occupancy optimization, and cluster operations at scale.

Presents an AI-native asset classification approach combining deterministic rules with an LLM fallback, discussing ground-truth improvement loops, active learning, LLM-as-Judge evaluation, and distilling LLM behavior into auditable rules.
Details the evolution of Meta's storage architecture to optimize GPU utilization and research velocity, addressing rapid growth in model capabilities and dataset sizes.
Live panel discussion on systems and reliability for AI infrastructure.
Meta's Head of Production Engineering presents on infrastructure for AI through agentic innovation and the future of autonomous reliability.
Documents a March 2025 incident where Meta avoided a major outage by re-bootstrapping the infrastructure control plane in an entire region within minutes using emergency tooling.
Examines Microsoft's SSH debugging across global Kubernetes cluster fleets, integrating just-in-time elevations and task-based execution with AI and Semantic Kernel for on-call problem resolution.
Addresses NCCL watchdog timeouts in distributed training, describing investigation across Meta's recommendation systems, categorization of root causes, and agent-based debugging approaches.

Enhancing Runtime Reliability in LLM Training via Fine-Grained Observability. All right, thank you. I'm Le Jang, a research scientist at BYU. I work on reliability and performance analysis for our AI infrastructure or RDMA.

Today, I'm going to talk about our recent work on improving the large language model training reliability at runtime. Today's large language model training jobs spend tens of thousands of GPUs and often runs for days or even weeks.

At this scale, the system becomes extremely sensitive. Even small glinches can have outside impact. Training efficiency is fragile. A single fault, whether the hardware, software or networking related, can derail the entire job of silently waste compute.

Metrics can vary across workloads and hardware and different faults manifest differently on workloads. Failures often propagate indirectly. A root cause on one machine might appear as symptoms somewhere else.

Locks and traces are often caused missing necessary information to reveal the actual root cause. And when issues do happen, manual diagnosis is slow, often requiring deep expertise and coordination across multiple teams.

All of these points to the need for finer grain observability. In this talk, I'm going to introduce our solutions to leverage different fine grain observabilities to improve reliability in our daily large language model training systems.

First, I'll talk about how we leverage metrics for efficient and accurate debugging. Even though we have many collected metrics, using them may not be treatable. Let's take a look at an example of PCIe downgrading and justify how such process is tricky.

When the PCIe downgrading issue occur at a receiver nick, it caused the nick to reduce its data transmission rate. As a result, the nick buffer quickly filled up triggering the nick to send more PFC signals to the transmitter side.

Before the transmitter could adjust and slow down its transmission, the switch buffer become overwhelmed by infrared packets leading to an increase in the ECN signals. Consequently, the CMP at the fault technique search as well.

When system developers and operators try to debug the problem, it could be confusing to figure out the real root cause among all those problematic metrics. Through our analysis, we identified several challenges in designing an accurate fing machine detection systems.

First, faults can occur across a wide range of areas from intrahost computation to inter host networks and from hardware to software communication libraries. These faults are diverse in both type and proportion.

When examining faults and their corresponding matrix, we observe that the same fault type can manifest as different anomalies in matrix. For instance, an ECC error might result in both high GPU or CPU utilization.

On the other way, the same metric anomaly does not always point to the same fault. For example, when we see high GPU utilization, it could be either associated with GPU hardware errors, software errors, or even some network issues.

Besides defining whether a behavior is anomaly are actually task dependent. For example, a GPU temperature of seven Celsius degrees is considered abnormal when the GPU clock frequency is 1350 MHz, but is normal for tasks when GPU operate at a higher clock frequency of 1800 MHz.

Additionally, we observe that metric data often contains noises caused by factors such as jeters, inaccurate sensors, timestamp misalignment, and more. These inconsistencies can mislead the detection process, making it less reliable.
We have seen that it's not trivial to leverage those collected metrics to debug complex problems. Then let's think about how to utilize the correlation among those metrics.

We first find that matrix across machines as with consistent behavior during parallel distributed training at per second observation level. When a fault occurs such as PC down grading, the PFC rate demonstrates distinctive differences compared to normal operation.
This principle can be extended to other metrics offering a potential pathway for systematically identifying the fs. To denise the time series data, we can leverage the notion of abnormal continuity.
Once a failure occur, abnormal performance typically persists across seven consecutive windows per second. This can be used as a reliable indicator for identifying faulty machines and ruout noises.

To address task dependent anomalies, we find that we can use individual models to represent each monitoring metric. Since the monitoring metric are independent, these models and their respective metric data are utilized independently for both denoising and detection purposes.

So with those insights, we built Minder, a fault detection system for large language model training. Minder processes metric data for each time window and generates an alert when a faulty machine is detected. The design consists of two key components.

constructing a VAE based parametric model for anomaly detection and a similarity and continuity based check to ensure accurate fault identification to construct a parametric models because labeling task dependent faults is actually challenging and may not be practical.

We choose to use unsupervised learning. VA is widely used for denoising and compressing high dimensional features with its strong uh reconstruction capabilities and is proved to be effective option in mind.
In mind, we deploy LSTM as both encoder and decoder given our data consists of temporal time series.

LSTM is also shown suitable in our cases as it considers both forward and backward information enabling it to extract a complete correspondence information from the data with the constructed parametric models.

Minder achieves runtime detection that applies the concept of machine level machine level similarity and continuity. As from our insights, for each time window, minder first prep-processes the route data and use the pre-trained model for reconstruction.
It then compares the similarity among all the machines by analyzing the score of the same metric. It in the presence of a 40 machine, the Z score is significantly higher than a predefined threshold.

Wonder then shifts the time window and identifies machines that consistently as bit anomalies across consecutive windows as faulty thereby filtering out false alarms. One important thing here is the metric prioritization.

Minder calculates maximum Z score of a metric across all machines and maintains a decision tree on metric selection. Minder has been successfully deployed in BYU monitoring task at a scale of over machines.
In our evaluation, Minder reduces detection time by 99% compared to previous manual diagnosis processes.
We compared the minder with baseline auto detection method and it demonstrates minders performance by effectively detecting actual faults while maintaining a lower false alarm rate.
We analyzed the breakdown of four types and for that minder achieves an overall precision of 90% effectively handling CPU and GPU related errors.

From our detection results, we also discover that models focusing on PFC CPU and GPU related metrics are sufficient to address the majority of faults. So you've so far seen how to leverage matrix to identify anomalies in large scale training.

Unfortunately, real world bugs could be even complex where the magics are not enough to expose problems. Now, let's look at a different level of observability which we found was previously missing but important. The collective communication library.

Some of the most frustrating failures we see in large scale training aren't crashes. They are great failures. In these cases, the jobs throw down or even get stuck. But none of the system metrics raise an obvious flag.

Everything looks fine on the dashboard, but the iteration rate jobs and the job suddenly with compute. Such great failure can be from a single GPU's abnormal behaviors in collective communication which can cascade to the entire job. Here's an example that shows the blind spot.

When engineers look at the profiler output, nothing captured buggy information. In the end, identifying the faulty GPU required manual testing and elimination, which is pretty timeconuming.

This kind of gray failure is especially dangerous because communication libraries today operate as black boxes boxes. There's no insight into where and why communication get stuck. Many tracing tools have been deployed across the stack to aid performance analysis and debugging.

At the operation level, tools like Chara can capture execution timelines and provide cost insight into training workloads. At the kernel level, frameworks such as NSITE and MBKE offer deeper instrumentation of CUDA kernel activity.
However, when collective communication problem occurs, these tools fall into many limitations. First, there's a granularity mismatch.

Cost grain tools lack the resolution to capture styles within an operation while lower level tools provide overwhelming details which actually mislead the debugging process. Another concern is the scalability and efficiency.
Many tools are designed for offline testing which is not suitable for runtime detection given their overhead and functionality. Lastly, most tools offer limited context for root cause analysis.

They capture symptoms but do not aid in locating the fault to a specific system or hardware component. We have seen that CCL level information is necessary to construct the end observability and further expose important runtime information.

But also we've discussed that achieving such ob observability at runtime is hard. Our idea is to review the dependencies, a key aspect for collective communication and see how this can help our debugging process.

We argue that we should think of the CCL as a sim as a system software, not just a library of functions. Especially for collective communications, we can think about the overall data transmission process as a global state machine.
Our GPUs move through stages to transmit and sync data in different parallelisms which involves strong control and data dependencies. We conclude these dependencies at different levels.

ranks in a group depend on each other for data synchronization where downstream data transmission relies on completed upstream data coming in. Besides operations like reduce scatter itself involves both communication and computation which can also involve further dependencies.

By identifying those dependencies, we can trace them at different levels. We first trace trunk level information where we monitor how data transmission is achieved along an operations execution. This includes how much data is copied and transmitted at runtime.

On different dimension, we track flow level information where we observe how multiple network passes are transmitting data in parallel which involves how many channels and QPS at the at the same time.

Tracing those dependencies can provide further observability on how collective communication executes across our GPUs at runtime to understand what's the system state when a failure or performance problem occur with the idea of tracing dependencies in collective communication.
We build Minecraft, a tracing system that achieves CCL level observability and root cause analysis again at runtime.

We instrument the Nikos proxy to capture runtime information by tracking trunk movement through the communication pass including when data is copied, transmission and completed. Such instrumentation covers all network passes that achieve the data transmission.
Microsoft generally collects two types of the logs. Completion log are generated at the end of each collective summarizing metadata such as start and time, total data transferred and nick usage.

State logs are emitted periodically during the operation recording per component trunk progress effectively producing a time resolved view of communication behavior especially when the latency is longer than expected.

All generated logs are written to pre-allocated memory space at runtime which significantly reduce the overhead injected to critical pass. All data collection, uploading and further analysis are on the side of the main process. This enables Microoft to get runtime data at scale.

Once data is collected, Microsoft is deployed in the back end to consume all those data for detection and root cause analysis. A practical question is how to quickly detect problems among a large scale cluster.

Macrooft's trigger triggering mechanism is based on the observation that with complex parallelisms failures or performance problems can rapidly spread across all GPUs. Our experience is that such cascading is usually within a few seconds.
Given this only monitoring a subset of GPUs is enough to implement a proactive trigger mechanism and quickly identify a new problem.

We check for many type of failures or performance issues and use this to determine potential problems occurred in collective communication where we should analyze the root cause. Once an anomaly is triggered, Microsoft runs a lightweight dependencydriven root cause analysis.

The logic varies based on the communication patterns. In ring based operations, we compare how much data each rank has already transmitted. The rank with the least progress is often the source of the stock.
We also look at the trunk level state to determine what type of problem occurred. For example, if data was prepared but never transmitted, that likely points to a network fault.

If everything was transmitted but GPU activity is stuck, that's likely a compute set issue and we should figure out why the GPU didn't copy data. This gives operators not just a failure alert but the direction of the deeper root causes.
We implemented Microoft as an always on system that tracks Bance daily training jobs. Microoft is a lightweight system and can be deployed on a single machine that has only 64 CPUs.

At runtime, Microsoft only requires reasonable amount of memory to monitor all large scale training tasks in our company. Despite at this scale, the footprint on each training host is minimal. Microoft only involves limited CPU usage and fix 512 megabytes shared memory.
This allows Miccraft to run with low overhead without affecting the training performance. From our experience, Microcraft makes no observable MFU difference for our online jobs.
We also evaluate Microcraft on a test bed across a run of workflows and we found no observable differences in end to end training time between runs with and without Microsoft enabled.
Instead of runtime deployment, we also conducted a series of faulty injection imper experiments to verify Microsoft's ability.

We injected various type of failures into training jobs such as slowing down arches, starting GPU operations, blocking the nic proxy or congesting the network. These faults were introduced on our test bed. The results were promising.

Microsoft successfully detected all faults and correctly localized the root cause in all those cases. Here's an example. We simulated a nick shutdown during the training. At runtime, we disable the argument on the review because Niko doesn't immediately crash in this case.

The job just gets stuck as expected with no clear errors in logs or other professors output. Microsoft quickly picked up the anomaly. It noticed that a rank stopped sending prepared data through nick while others are also stuck but has transmitted more data.

It correctly localized the problem to the problematic GPU and identified the nick as the potential failing component. This process was completed in just a few seconds way before the job would have timed out.

So to wrap up, large language model training demands not just the efficiency but reliability at runtime. In this talk, we show how traditional tools fall short. They are either too high level or too heavy to help when failures silently propagate at runtime.
Our solution was to effectively leverage different level of observabilities either utilizing the metrics or instrumenting necessary trace points to collect the meaningful runtime system states.
We also emphasize our argument that CCL should not be operated as a black box and we actually can observe it in lightweight methods.

We believe that bringing system level observability closer to machine learning infrastructure will be essential as training continues to scale or at different machine learning scenarios like inferencing both to reduce waste and to improve robustness in production environments.

We also believe that our work is just a starting point that opens the door to a broader set of techniques that can meaningfully advance long-term reliability in machine learning infrastructures. All right, by this I want to thank you for coming to the talk.

How Meta Scaled AI Training Storage via Data Normalization. Hello everyone. My name is Weidong Liu. With my colleague Saran, we will we will walk you through the power sequence storage which is scared our training data for the next generation recommender system models.

For recommender systems, training data storage is an industry-wide challenge. Models desire exabyte scale data to make better predictions. And scaling the training storage becomes the top priority for the big recommender systems as we seen in these papers.

Over the past years, Meta invested heavily into training storage. We have built critical optimizations to keep up with faster scale growth. Our work can be categorized into four categories: columnar compression, row reordering, column reordering, and the model aware placement.

We exploit data similarities and the common access patterns to compress the data better and the minimize physical data copies. This optimization work well for the training data with traditional features like dense and sparse features before a new data paradigm appeared.

The new data paradigm is user sequence and it changed everything. So, what is a user sequence? It consists of users' raw history of interactions from months to years. It encodes richer user behaviors, content understanding data, and the contextual information.

With high data density, user sequences are 100 x or more larger than traditional features. On the modeling side, Meta pivoted to learn from user sequences over the past 3 years for both advertisement and organic content recommendations.

We published the market for papers that reported the effectiveness on boosting our 12 AI models. We have seen steadily and the significant model quality improvements as user sequence lens grows to be longer.

Back to the data side, user sequence with 100 x larger size than traditional features becomes a single dominant driving factor of training data storage from 2023. Even with all the previous storage optimization mentioned, the growth actually further accelerated.

Now, training data is of tens exabytes stored in more than 10 data centers and cost the 10% of meta's power budget. Moreover, in addition to the high cost, this exabyte scale data list many other challenges.
First, due to the data center capacity constraints, we are forced to down sample all the time and up front throw valuable training samples away.
For the same reason, in many cases, we're only able to keep training samples for 1 month and the much longer term knowledge like seasonality is lost. In the end, we're experimenting new data formats.

We need to process and generate huge volume of data, which is both time-consuming and costly. So far, we have discussed that we engineered a strong solution for traditional training data features. However, user sequences change the landscape.

Now, I'm here to share a bit more about the challenges with handling user sequence with our current stack and how we tackle these challenges. But before we go there, I wanted to talk about some basic concepts and a key observation that led to the solution.

Let's start with a training row. A training row is a sample that logged for training at the time of inference and captures the complete snapshot of the ML feature used during inference along with the label that sort of indicates how good the prediction was.

A training table is a collection of training rows logged over a certain time period. A training preprocessor reads these training rows, reconstructs the training samples, feeds it to the GPU at the time of training.
Our previous data model for training relied on fully self-contained rows. That is, each training row stored a complete snapshot of the features that were used at the time of inference.

This makes it very easy to reconstruct the training sample at the time of training because all we need to do is read the training row and nothing else. However, there's a downside with this approach, which is data duplication.

When we analyze the training data across all our training tables, we find a lot of data duplication. The reason this happens is ML features between training rows that are fairly close to each other in time do not change much and in fact in many cases, they're exactly the same.

As a result, there's a lot of data duplication. And this design choice was intentional in the past when our training growth size was small. Since the ML features were highly condensed summaries capturing user behavior and interactions.

With user sequences though, the nature of training data completely changed. There was suddenly a desire to store a lot more information about the user interactions and behavior over a much longer time period. Now the training growth was suddenly 100x larger.

As a result, our previous data model was no longer scaling and we needed to revisit some of these fundamental assumptions. So what was our solution over here? Our solution to this problem is normalizing training data.

And normalization is a very well researched pattern in the relational database world. And it's very relevant here as well. The idea on a very high level is pretty simple. Rather than storing all of the training data in a single table, we split the data into two tables.

Specifically, the user sequences, which were duplicated and stored in line in each of the training growth previously, are now moved to a separate table called a user sequence table where each user sequence is addressed by a unique user sequence ID.
The original training table containing training growth now no longer store these features in line within the row, but store a lightweight pointer to the rows in the sequence table.
With this, we can reduce data duplication significantly, but however, there is a trade-off over here. And the trade-off is there is a more complex training sample reconstruction.

For reconstructing training samples now, the training preprocessor needs to do join reading a training row from the training table and the user sequence from the user sequence table to reconstruct these training samples on the fly at the time of training.

The entire solution becomes cost effective only if we are able to optimize these joins and we'll look into how we optimize these joints in a bit. Now, with that background, let's take a look at the solution for handling user sequences. Sequence storage.
Sequence storage is the system that we built for handling user sequences. And this is depicted pictorially as shown on the slide. Like I mentioned before, training data is now split into two tables.
The training table containing the training rows with lightweight references to keys in the user sequence table continues to remain on warehouse backed by warm storage HDD.

The user sequence table is moved to an efficient indexable storage since the access pattern we expect on this table to be point lookups. We decided to leverage ZippyDB, which is a widely used distributed key-value store within Meta for this indexable storage.

On the train preprocessor side, we now needed a special connector that does a join between the training table and the user sequence table to reconstruct these training samples at the time of training.
The first version of sequence storage that we built and integrated into our training pipeline turned out to be a lot more inefficient than we expected it to be.

On the storage server side, we were looking at a major read throughput optimization challenge with a very aggressive target. Overall, we wanted to reduce the cost per gigabyte per second of training throughput that we could get from the storage server.
And we identified three major sources of inefficiencies that we had to optimize in order to hit our throughput goals. On a very high level, these inefficiencies were high read amplification.

We noticed that the storage servers had higher read amplification for AI training workloads. As a result, this limited the effective throughput that we could get from the storage servers and we had to land a lot of optimizations to reduce this amplification.

The second one was high read latency due to the index join. We had to optimize the index join to reduce the latency and remove any stalls in our data pipeline and ensure the GPUs were well utilized. And finally, there was a problem of data over fetching.

Both factors combined severely limit the storage server read throughput because we need to budget a substantial amount of resources on serving writes as well as we much amplify the flash IO usage per read.
For this challenge, we tackle it by introducing a new offloaded SSTable compaction and optimization pass.

In the VDDV, we introduced a new API that allows direct load pre-generated SSTables from external completely bypassing the expensive memtable and the write-ahead logs in the server from the row-by-row insertion pass.

Moreover, we make our query engines like Spark and Presto to recognize SSTable files. On the top, we are able to build data pipelines to periodically compact, optimize, and index data outside of the VDDV servers. Once everything is done, we have our final SSTable files.

We are able to load the newly generated files into VDDV servers and the replace the older ones. This resulted with a single look structured the merge tree optimized for read. Every single read now is only served from a single SST table file.

We pretty much achieved one IO per read. For the second challenge, high read latency, we looked closely to be that the scans were executed in serial. This was because RocksDB doesn't support native multiple scans and ZippyDB had to do the scans serially.

With that implementation, when querying for batch of user sequences, the total latency was the sum of all individual scans. This high latency led to training GPU QPS regression risks since we need to wait longer for fetching user sequences.

Our solution was to implement native multi-scan implementation in RocksDB. When a batch of reads are received via a new multi-scan API, RocksDB first taking sight into adjacent scan ranges to identify IO coalescing opportunities.

Meaning like if two IOs are hitting the adjacent disk blocks and whether we can do two instead of doing two scans, we do one scan instead. After identifying the coalesced ranges, we issue those IOs in parallel against these ranges.

This reduced the storage media IO IOPS and improved the query latency. Now, the total latency is only decided by the single slowest scan from multiple scans. And therefore, this reduced the latency, increased the training GPU QPS. Last challenge was data over fetching.

Architecting Infrastructure for the AI Native Future: Scaling Autonomous Agents on Google TPUs. The rise of AI agents is pushing the boundaries of AI infrastructure. With the autonomous nature of these agents, they require continuous execution.
Think about thousands of agents that require massive throughput in AI accelerators, which is the foundational enabler for continuous real-time autonomy for complex AI agents.

Additionally, because these AI agents are operating on long horizon tasks, it becomes critical to enable vast context windows. These are critical memory workings that grounds AI agents, enabling deep situational awareness and the execution of complex long horizon tasks.

Last but not least, there is a real-time reasoning component to AI agents. They require AI infrastructure that provides high-speed inference and low latency memory access that is necessary for continuous real-time agentic orchestration.
These demands, we're seeing them across the entire model development life cycle of the agentic era. Starting with pre-training the mixture of experts models that drive these AI agents.
Models are rising up to the 10 trillion parameter sizes, and this requires optimized hardware that is specialized for high throughput pre-training.

And even before you launch the model for serving, we're seeing the rise of reinforcement learning techniques like sampling, where the model goes through several iterations of reasoning loops to make it more efficient and more accurate.
And then you serve hundreds of thousands of AI agents that are continuously prompting and waiting for responses, requiring efficiency in latency.

With these demands across this entire AI agentic development life cycle, we're seeing that the bottleneck for innovation has shifted from just being algorithmic design efficiency to being both algorithmic design and the underlying AI infrastructure.
That's why today, I would like to talk about architecting infrastructure for the AI native future, specifically focusing on how you can scale autonomous agents on Google's TPUs.

When you think about specialized hardware for AI ML workloads, you've got CPUs that are generalist and serial thinkers. So, while they're versatile, they're not very specialized for one specific tasks. They're very flexible, but they're bottlenecked by high throughput tasks.

And then on the other hand, you've got GPUs. They're also generalist, but they're parallel thinkers. They excel at parallel tasks, like graphics and AI ML tasks. They use massive parallelism to prevent any throughput bottlenecks. Now, TPUs on the other hand, they're specialist.
